items.q: What is the range of integers an 8-bit two's complement number can store?
items.correct: -128 to +127: the leading bit carries weight -128, so the negative side reaches one further than the positive side.
items.misconception: -127 to +127, because the range must be symmetrical around zero.
items.random_wrong: 0 to 255, the same as an unsigned byte.
items.why: Students assume symmetry. There is only one zero in two's complement, freeing one extra pattern for the negatives, hence the asymmetric -128 to +127.

items.q: What comes after F when counting in hexadecimal?
items.correct: 10 (one-zero), which represents denary 16; the digits restart and a new place value column begins, just as 9 rolls over to 10 in denary.
items.misconception: G - the alphabet simply continues after F.
items.random_wrong: F1 - you append a 1 to the F.
items.why: Students extend the letters instead of rolling over. Hex has exactly sixteen symbols (0-9, A-F); after the last symbol the place-value system takes over.

items.q: Does doubling the colour depth of an image double the number of available colours?
items.correct: No - each extra BIT doubles the colours, so going from 8-bit to 16-bit multiplies the available colours by 2^8 = 256, not by 2.
items.misconception: Yes - twice the bits means twice the colours.
items.random_wrong: No - colour depth has no effect on the number of colours.
items.why: The exponential relationship is missed. Colours = 2^depth, so depth changes act in the exponent: small bit increases produce huge colour increases.
It's 26. “16” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: In 8-bit two's complement representation, a leading 1 indicates that the number is:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Negative. “Even” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.correct: Can represent characters from far more of the world's writing systems
items.misconception: Uses fewer bits for every character
items.why: Correct answer: Can represent characters from far more of the world's writing systems. “Uses fewer bits for every character” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: The colour depth of a bitmap image is:
items.correct: The number of bits used to represent each pixel
items.misconception: The number of pixels in the image
items.random_wrong: The physical size of the screen
items.why: Correct answer: The number of bits used to represent each pixel. “The number of pixels in the image” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: A bitmap is 100 by 100 pixels with a colour depth of 8 bits per pixel. Ignoring metadata, what is the file size in bytes?
items.why: Exact answer: 10000 bytes. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
